title: STANDARD FOR CHILLI PEPPERS CXS 307-2011 Adopted in 2011.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR CHILLI PEPPERS CXS 307-2011 Adopted in 2011.
sections.summary: Document title: Codex Standard for Chilli Peppers (CXS 307‑2011), adopted 2011.
Scope: Standard applies to commercial fresh chilli peppers (Capsicum spp.) supplied to consumers after preparation and packaging and excludes chilli peppers for industrial processing.

Provisions concerning quality: sets minimum requirements (soundness, firmness, cleanliness, freedom from pests, abnormal moisture, foreign tastes/odours and temperature damage, maturity/transport suitability), and defines three quality classes (Extra, I, II) with allowable superficial defect areas of up to 0.5%, 2.0% and 3.0% respectively.

Minimum requirements (general): chilli peppers must be whole (stem may be missing if break is clean), sound, firm, clean, practically free of pests and visible foreign matter, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign smell/taste, and free of temperature damage.

sections.section: ## 2.1.1 Chilli  peppers must be harvested at an appropriate degree of development in accordance with the criteria proper to the variety and the area in which they are grown.
sections.summary: Harvest maturity and handling: chilli peppers must be harvested at an appropriate stage for the variety/area so they withstand transport and handling and arrive in satisfactory condition.
Classification overview: chilli peppers are classified into three classes (Extra, I, II) with progressively looser defect tolerances and requirements as detailed in subsections.

'Extra' Class: chilli peppers of superior quality, characteristic of the variety, free of defects except very slight superficial defects affecting up to 0.5% of surface that do not impair appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I: chilli peppers of good quality, characteristic of the variety, with slight defects allowed affecting up to 2.0% of surface provided they do not affect general appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class II: includes chilli peppers not qualifying for higher classes but meeting minimum requirements, with defects allowed up to 3.0% of surface provided essential characteristics for quality, keeping and presentation are retained.
Sizing: size of chilli peppers is determined by length in centimetres according to a five‑code table (code 1: ≤4 cm; 2: 4–8; 3: 8–12; 4: 12–16; 5: >16 cm).

Tolerances: permits quality and size tolerances per package—quality tolerances for Extra (5%), Class I (10%), Class II (10% non‑conforming except rotting), and size tolerance of 10% corresponding to adjacent size categories.
Size tolerances: for all classes up to 10% by number or weight may be chilli peppers corresponding to the size immediately above and/or below that indicated on the package.

Presentation provisions: requires package uniformity (same origin, quality, size, variety, visible portion representative), protective packaging in new/clean food‑grade materials, compliance with CXC 44‑1995, and containers meeting hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements.
Uniformity: each package must contain only chilli peppers of the same origin, quality, size and variety and the visible contents must represent the entire package.

Packaging requirements: chilli peppers must be packed to protect the produce using new or food‑grade recycled clean materials that will not damage produce, with containers complying with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44‑1995) and free of foreign matter or smell.

Marking/labelling: consumer and non‑retail packages must meet CXS 1‑1985 plus specific provisions on declaring produce name/variety/pungency, and non‑retail containers must carry identification, nature, origin and commercial identification details, with an optional official inspection mark.
Consumer package labelling: in addition to general Codex labelling, if produce is not externally visible the package must state the name and variety and may state pungency level.
Nature of produce labelling: if contents are not visible the package must be labelled with the name of the produce and the variety and may include level of pungency.

Non‑retail container marking: requires legible, indelible particulars visible from outside or in accompanying documents, including identification, nature, origin and commercial identification details.
Identification marking: non‑retail packages must show name and address of exporter/packer/dispatcher and may include an identification code.
Nature of produce (non‑retail): non‑retail packages must state the produce name if not visible, the variety, and optionally the level of pungency.
Origin marking: non‑retail packages must declare country of origin and may optionally specify district or regional/local place name.
Commercial identification: labels should indicate class, size, variety and pungency.
Official inspection mark: inclusion of an official inspection mark on packaging is optional.
Contaminants: chilli peppers must comply with maximum levels set in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins (CXS 193‑1995) and with Codex pesticide maximum residue limits.

sections.section: 7.1 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum levels of the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995).
sections.summary: Contaminant limits: produce must comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995).

sections.section: 7.2 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum residue limits for pesticides established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.
sections.summary: Pesticide residues: produce must comply with Codex‑established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Hygiene: recommends preparing and handling produce in accordance with Codex hygienic texts (CXC 1‑1969, CXC 53‑2003 and relevant Codes), and complying with any Codex microbiological criteria per CXG 21‑1997.

sections.section: 8.1 It is recommended that the produce covered by the provisions of this Standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003), and other relevant Codex texts such as Codes of Hygienic Practice and Codes of Practice.
sections.summary: Hygienic practice recommendation: produce should be prepared and handled per the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969), Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53‑2003) and other relevant Codex texts.

sections.section: 8.2 The produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).
sections.summary: Microbiological criteria: produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with CXG 21‑1997.
